revision.comment: trans from ja; need an expert to check this, especially terminology

revision.text:
'''Ugayafukiaezu''' (鵜葺草葺不合命) is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]). In [[Kojiki]] the name appears as 天津日高日子波限建鵜草葦不合命 (Amatsuhitakahiko nakisatake ugayafukiaezu no mikoto), and in [[Nihonshoki]] 彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊 (Hikonagisatake ugayafukiaezu no mikoto).
Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] and the daughter of the [[Ryujin]] (sea god) [[Toyotamabina]]. Although Toyotamabina became pregnant at [[Ryugu]], the sea shrine, she rose to the land, for she thought that she ought not to bear the child of the Tenjin (heaven's god) in the sea. Hoori and Toyotamabina tried to build a house for delivering in the beach, but while they were finishing roofing the house with the feather of cormorant instead of saw grass, Toyotamabina began labor. So the child was named Ugayafukiaezu (lit. the roofing cormorant alternative to saw grass was not in time). In delivering she said to Hoori "a person in other countries goes back to the original form when delivering a child. Because I think I too am going back to the original form, do not look at the house no matter what, while I am delivering." However, Hoori looked in the house and saw [[Yahirowani]] (shark), her original form, so he ran away. Toyotamabina felt shameful and returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayoribina]] instead. While helding a grudge against him, Toyotamabina missed Hoori as she loved him, so she communicated with him via her sister.
Later, Ugayafukiaezu got married with Tamayoribina, who rose him, and they had four children [[Itsuse]], [[Inahi]], [[Mikenu]] and [[Emperor Jimmu of Japan|Wakemikenu]]. Mikenu traveled to [[Tokoyo]] (lit. the normal world; some point that it is afterworld), and Inahi went to the ocean where his (her?) mother is. Wakamikenu, the youngest, became [[Emperor Jimmu of Japan|Emperor Jimmu]] afterward.
[[ja:鵜葺草葺不合命]] {{Japan-stub}}

[[fr:Ugayafukiaezu]] [[ja:ウガヤフキアエズ]]

'''Ugayafukiaezu''' (鵜葺草葺不合命) is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]). In the [[Kojiki]] his name appears as "天津日高日子波限建鵜草葦不合命 " (Amatsuhitakahiko nakisatake ugayafukiaezu no mikoto), and in the [[Nihonshoki]] he is called "彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊" (Hikonagisatake ugayafukiaezu no mikoto). In [[Japanese mythology]], Ugayafukiaezu is the father of Japan's (mythical) first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]].
Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] and the daughter of the [[Ryujin]] (sea god) [[Toyotamabime]]. Although Toyotamabime became pregnant at [[Ryugu]], the sea shrine, she rose to the land, for she thought that she ought not to bear the child of the Tenjin (heaven's god) in the sea. Hoori and Toyotamabime tried to build a house for delivering the baby on the beach, building the roof with feathers of cormorant instead of saw grass. However, while they were finishing the roof, Toyotamabime went into labor. So the child was named Ugayafukiaezu (lit. the roofing cormorant alternative to saw grass was not in time).
While in labor, Toyotamabime said to Hoori, "people in other countries change back to their original form when delivering a child. Because I think I too am changing back to my original form, do not look in the house no matter what, while I am delivering the child." However, Hoori looked in the house and saw Toyotamabime in her original form of [[Yahirowani]] (a shark), and he fled. Toyotamabime felt shameful and returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayoribime]] to raise the child in her place. While holding a grudge against him, Toyotamabime loved and missed Hoori, so she communicated with him via her sister.
Later, Ugayafukiaezu married Tamayoribime, and they had four children [[Itsuse]], [[Inahi]], [[Mikenu]] and [[Emperor Jimmu|Wakemikenu]]. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo (lit. the normal world; some say that it is the afterworld), and Inahi went to the ocean to be with his mother. Wakamikenu, their youngest child, would later become [[Emperor Jimmu]].
{{Japan-stub}} {{Asia-myth-stub}}
{{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]
[[fr:Ugayafukiaezu]] [[ja:ウガヤフキアエズ]]

'''Ugayafukiaezu''' (鵜葺草葺不合命) is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]). In [[Japanese mythology]], Ugayafukiaezu is the father of Japan's (mythical) first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. In the [[Kojiki]] his name appears as "天津日高日子波限建鵜草葦不合命 " (Amatsuhitakahiko nakisatake ugayafukiaezu no mikoto), and in the [[Nihonshoki]] he is called "彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊" (Hikonagisatake ugayafukiaezu no mikoto).
Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] and [[Toyotamabime]], who was the daughter of [[Ryujin]] (the sea god). Although Toyotamabime became pregnant at [[Ryugu]], the sea shrine, afterwards Toyotamabime went to land, for she thought that she ought not to bear the child of the Tenjin (heaven's god) in the sea. Hoori and Toyotamabime tried to build a house for delivering the baby on the beach, building the roof with feathers of cormorant instead of saw grass. However, while they were finishing the roof, Toyotamabime went into labor. So the child was named Ugayafukiaezu (lit. the roofing cormorant alternative to saw grass was not in time).
While in labor, Toyotamabime said to Hoori, "people in other countries change back to their original form when delivering a child. Because I think I too am changing back to my original form, do not look in the house no matter what, while I am delivering the child." However, Hoori looked in the house and saw Toyotamabime in her original form of [[Yahirowani]] (a shark), and he fled. Toyotamabime felt shameful and returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayoribime]] to raise the child in her place. While holding a grudge against him, Toyotamabime loved and missed Hoori, so she communicated with him via her sister.
Later, Ugayafukiaezu married Tamayoribime, and they had four children [[Itsuse]], [[Inahi]], [[Mikenu]] and [[Emperor Jimmu|Wakemikenu]]. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo (lit. the normal world; some say that it is the afterworld), and Inahi went to the ocean to be with his mother. Wakamikenu, their youngest child, would later become [[Emperor Jimmu]].

'''Ugayafukiaezu''' (鵜葺草葺不合命) is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]). In [[Japanese mythology]], Ugayafukiaezu is the father of Japan's (mythical) first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. In the [[Kojiki]] his name appears as "天津日高日子波限建鵜草葦不合命 " (Amatsuhitakahiko nakisatake ugayafukiaezu no mikoto), and in the [[Nihonshoki]] he is called "彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊" (Hikonagisatake ugayafukiaezu no mikoto).

Toyotamabime felt shameful and returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayoribime]] to raise the child in her place.

[[fr:Ugayafukiaezu]] [[ja:ウガヤフキアエズ]]

{{nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu'''|鵜葺草葺不合命|}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]). In [[Japanese mythology]], Ugayafukiaezu is the father of Japan's (mythical) first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. In the ''[[Kojiki]]'' his name appears as '''Amatsuhitakahiko-Nakisatake-Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto''' (天津日高日子波限建鵜草葦不合命), and in the ''[[Nihonshoki]]'' he is called '''Hikonagisatake-Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto''' (彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊).
Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] and [[Toyotama-hime]], who was the daughter of [[Ryujin]] (the sea god). Although Toyotama-hime became pregnant at [[Ryugu]], the sea shrine, afterwards she went to land, for she thought that she ought not to bear the child of the Tenjin (heaven's god) in the sea. Hoori and Toyotama-hime tried to build a house for delivering the baby on the beach, building the roof with feathers of cormorant instead of saw grass. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. So the child was named Ugayafukiaezu (lit. the roofing cormorant alternative to saw grass was not in time).
While in labor, Toyotama-hime said to Hoori, "people in other countries change back to their original form when delivering a child. Because I think I too am changing back to my original form, do not look in the house no matter what, while I am delivering the child." However, Hoori looked in the house and saw Toyotama-hime in her original form of [[Yahirowani]] (a shark), and he fled. Feeling shamed, she returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayori-hime]] to raise the child in her place.
Later, when Ugayafukiaezu married Tamayori-hime, and they had four children: [[Itsuse]], [[Inahi]], [[Mikenu]], and [[Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]]. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo (lit. the normal world; some say that it is the afterworld), and Inahi went to the ocean to be with his mother. Kamuyamato Iwarebiko, their youngest child, would later become Emperor Jimmu.
{{Japan-stub}} {{Asia-myth-stub}}

[[fr:Ugayafukiaezu]] [[ja:ウガヤフキアエズ]]

{{nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命|}}, commonly shortened to '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]) and in [[Japanese mythology]], he is the father of Japan's (mythical) first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. In the [[Shinto]] [[sacred]] writing ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as '''Amatsuhitakahiko-Nakisatake-Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto''' (天津日高日子波限建鵜草葦不合命), and in the ''[[Nihonshoki]]'' he is called '''Hikonagisatake-Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto''' (彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊).
Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] and [[Toyotama-hime]], who was the daughter of [[Ryujin]] (the sea god). Though Toyotama-hime became pregnant at [[Ryugu]], the undersea palace, she opted not the bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. Both Hoori and Toyotama-hime attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to contstruct the roof with feathers of cormorant instead of saw grass. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. So the child was named Ugayafukiaezu (lit. the roofing cormorant alternative to saw grass was not in time).
While in labor, Toyotama-hime said to Hoori, "people in other countries change back to their original form when delivering a child. Because I think I too am changing back to my original form, do not look in the house no matter what, while I am delivering the child." However, Hoori looked in the house and saw Toyotama-hime in her original form of [[yahirowani|a shark]], and he fled. Feeling shamed, she returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayori-hime]] to raise the child in her place. Another take on the legend states that feeling completely humiliated, she rushed back to the sea, dropping the child on the shore; the cries of the newborn infant reached Tamayori-hime's ears and she opted to raise the child instead.
Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married Tamayori-hime, and they had four children: [[Itsuse|Itsuse-no-Mikoto]], [[Inahi|Inahi-no-Mikoto]], [[Mikenu|Mikenu-no-Mikoto]], and [[Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]]. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo (lit. "the normal world", though some texts hint that it is the afterworld), and Inahi went to the ocean to be with his mother. Kamuyamato Iwarebiko, their youngest child, would later become Emperor Jimmu.

Feeling shamed, she returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayori-hime]] to raise the child in her place. Another take on the legend states that feeling completely humiliated, she rushed back to the sea, dropping the child on the shore; the cries of the newborn infant reached Tamayori-hime's ears and she opted to raise the child instead.
Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married Tamayori-hime, and they had four children: [[Itsuse|Itsuse-no-Mikoto]], [[Inahi|Inahi-no-Mikoto]], [[Mikenu|Mikenu-no-Mikoto]], and [[Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]]. {{nihongo|'''Ugaya-fukiaezu-no-Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合 命|}}, commonly shortened to '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]) and in [[Japanese mythology]], he is the father of Japan's (mythical) first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. In ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as '''Amatsuhitaka-hiko'nakisa-takeugaya-fukiaezu-no-Mikoto''' (天津日高 日子波限 建鵜草葦不合 命), and in the ''[[Nihonshoki]]'' he is called '''Hiko'nagisa-takeugaya-fukiaezu-no-Mikoto''' (彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合 尊).
Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]], a son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] and [[Toyotama-hime]], a daughter of [[Ryujin]]. Though Toyotama-hime became pregnant at [[Ryugu]], the undersea palace, she opted not the bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. Both Hoori and Toyotama-hime attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to contstruct the roof with feathers of cormorant instead of saw grass. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. So the child was named Ugayafukiaezu (lit. the roofing cormorant alternative to saw grass was not in time).
While in labor, Toyotama-hime said to Hoori, "people in other countries change back to their original form when delivering a child. Because I think I too am changing back to my original form, do not look in the house no matter what, while I am delivering the child". However, Hoori looked in the house and saw Toyotama-hime in her original form of [[yahirowani|a shark]], and he fled. Feeling shamed, she returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayori-hime]] to raise the child in her place. Another take on the legend states that feeling completely humiliated, she rushed back to the sea, dropping the child on the shore; the cries of the newborn infant reached Tamayori-hime's ears and she opted to raise the child instead.
Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married Tamayori-hime, and they had four children: [[Itsuse|Itsuse-no-Mikoto]], [[Inahi|Inahi-no-Mikoto]], [[Mikenu|Mikenu-no-Mikoto]], and [[Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]]. Kamuyamato Iwarebiko, their youngest child, would later become Emperor Jimmu.

[[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]
[[es:Ugayafukiaezu]] [[fr:Ugayafukiaezu]] [[ja:ウガヤフキアエズ]]

{{nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命|}}, commonly shortened to '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]) and in [[Japanese mythology]], he is the father of Japan's (mythical) first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. In ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as '''Amatsuhitaka-hiko'nakisatake-ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto''' (天津日高日子波限 建鵜草葦不合命), and in the ''[[Nihonshoki]]'' he is called '''Hiko'nagisatake-ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto''' (彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊).
Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]], a son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and [[Toyotama-hime]], a daughter of [[Ryūjin]]. Though Toyotama-hime became pregnant at [[Ryūgū-jō|Ryūgū]], the undersea palace, she opted not the bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. Both Hoori and Toyotama-hime attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to contstruct the roof with feathers of cormorant instead of saw grass. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. So the child was named Ugayafukiaezu (lit. the roofing cormorant alternative to saw grass was not in time).
While in labor, Toyotama-hime said to Hoori, "people in other countries change back to their original form when delivering a child. Because I think I too am changing back to my original form, do not look in the house no matter what, while I am delivering the child". However, Hoori looked in the house and saw Toyotama-hime in her original form of [[yahirowani|a shark]], and he fled. Feeling shamed, she returned to the sea, leaving her newborn child behind and sending her younger sister [[Tamayori-hime]] to raise the child in her place. Another take on the legend states that feeling completely humiliated, she rushed back to the sea, dropping the child on the shore; the cries of the newborn infant reached Tamayori-hime's ears and she opted to raise the child instead.
Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married Tamayori-hime, and they had four children: [[Itsuse|Itsuse-no-Mikoto]], [[Inahi|Inahi-no-Mikoto]], [[Mikenu|Mikenu-no-Mikoto]], and [[Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]]. Mikenu traveled to ''Tokoyo'' (lit. "the normal world", though some texts hint that it is the afterworld), and Inahi went to the ocean to be with his mother. Kamuyamato Iwarebiko, their youngest child, would later become Emperor Jimmu.
==External references== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto]
{{jmyth navbox long}}
[[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

revision.comment: [[User:Addbot|Bot:]] Migrating 8 interwiki links, now provided by [[Wikipedia:Wikidata|Wikidata]] on [[d:q1373935]] ([[User talk:Addbot|Report Errors]])
Fixing spelling in accordance with [[MOS:JAPAN#Historical kana usage]].
/* top */ references, added a ton of modern translation work to clarify, added a photo
revision.text:
'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto''' "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" {{nihongo|鵜葺草葺不合命|}}{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|authorlink1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}}, commonly shortened to '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]), and in [[Japanese mythology]], he is the father of Japan's first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'' his name appears as {{nihongo|Seaside Brave Lad of Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching|天津日高日子波限 建鵜草葦不合命|ama tsu hikohiko nagisa take u ga ya fuki aezu no mikoto}} and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' the same name appears with a different spelling. Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] "Flickering Flame" (the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] "Ripening Rice-Ears Lad", who was sent by [[Amaterasu]] to rule the Earth), and of [[Toyotama-hime]] or "Lady Bountiful Soul", a daughter of Ocean Majesty (now identified as the dragon king, [[Ryūjin]]). Although Lady Bountiful Soul became pregnant at Ocean Majesty's undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, the parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, the Lady Bountiful Soul fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" because of her birthing hut. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, Tamayori-hime "Lady Summoned Soul", and they had four children: ''Itsuse'' "Hallowed Harvest", ''Inahi'' "Rice Spirit", ''Mikenu'' "Master Mighty Offerings and the future Emperor Jinmu, who had several names: Master Plentiful Mighty Offerings, Young Master Mighty Offerings and Boulder Hamlet Lad of Sacred Yamato. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inahi went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Itsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler of earth. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs= {{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033}} }} ==External references== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

revision.comment: [[WP:CHECKWIKI]] error fix for #61. Punctuation goes before References. Do [[Wikipedia:GENFIXES|general fixes]] if a problem exists. - using [[Project:AWB|AWB]] (10480)
revision.text:
'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto''' "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" {{nihongo|鵜葺草葺不合命|}},{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|authorlink1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} commonly shortened to '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]), and in [[Japanese mythology]], he is the father of Japan's first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'' his name appears as {{nihongo|Seaside Brave Lad of Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching|天津日高日子波限 建鵜草葦不合命|ama tsu hikohiko nagisa take u ga ya fuki aezu no mikoto}} and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' the same name appears with a different spelling. Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] "Flickering Flame" (the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] "Ripening Rice-Ears Lad", who was sent by [[Amaterasu]] to rule the Earth), and of [[Toyotama-hime]] or "Lady Bountiful Soul", a daughter of Ocean Majesty (now identified as the dragon king, [[Ryūjin]]). Although Lady Bountiful Soul became pregnant at Ocean Majesty's undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, the parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, the Lady Bountiful Soul fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" because of her birthing hut. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, Tamayori-hime "Lady Summoned Soul", and they had four children: ''Itsuse'' "Hallowed Harvest", ''Inahi'' "Rice Spirit", ''Mikenu'' "Master Mighty Offerings and the future Emperor Jinmu, who had several names: Master Plentiful Mighty Offerings, Young Master Mighty Offerings and Boulder Hamlet Lad of Sacred Yamato. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inahi went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Itsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler of earth. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs= {{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033}} }} ==External references== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

{{Infobox royalty | name = Ugayafukiaezu | reign = ? – 660 BC | birth_date = | birth_place = | death_date = | death_place = | place of burial = }} '''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto''' "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" {{nihongo|鵜葺草葺不合命|}},{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|authorlink1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} commonly shortened to '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]), and in [[Japanese mythology]], he is the father of Japan's first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'' his name appears as {{nihongo|Seaside Brave Lad of Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching|天津日高日子波限 建鵜草葦不合命|ama tsu hikohiko nagisa take u ga ya fuki aezu no mikoto}} and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' the same name appears with a different spelling. Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] "Flickering Flame" (the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] "Ripening Rice-Ears Lad", who was sent by [[Amaterasu]] to rule the Earth), and of [[Toyotama-hime]] or "Lady Bountiful Soul", a daughter of Ocean Majesty (now identified as the dragon king, [[Ryūjin]]). Although Lady Bountiful Soul became pregnant at Ocean Majesty's undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, the parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, the Lady Bountiful Soul fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" because of her birthing hut. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, Tamayori-hime "Lady Summoned Soul", and they had four children: ''Itsuse'' "Hallowed Harvest", ''Inahi'' "Rice Spirit", ''Mikenu'' "Master Mighty Offerings and the future Emperor Jinmu, who had several names: Master Plentiful Mighty Offerings, Young Master Mighty Offerings and Boulder Hamlet Lad of Sacred Yamato. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inahi went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Itsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler of earth. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs= {{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033}} }} ==External references== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto''' "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" {{nihongo|鵜葺草葺不合命|}},{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|authorlink1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} commonly shortened to '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[deity]] ([[kami]]), and in [[Japanese mythology]], he is the father of Japan's first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'' his name appears as {{nihongo|Seaside Brave Lad of Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching|天津日高日子波限 建鵜草葦不合命|ama tsu hikohiko nagisa take u ga ya fuki aezu no mikoto}} and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' the same name appears with a different spelling. Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] "Flickering Flame" (the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] "Ripening Rice-Ears Lad", who was sent by [[Amaterasu]] to rule the Earth), and of [[Toyotama-hime]] or "Lady Bountiful Soul", a daughter of Ocean Majesty (now identified as the dragon king, [[Ryūjin]]). Although Lady Bountiful Soul became pregnant at Ocean Majesty's undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, the parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, the Lady Bountiful Soul fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" because of her birthing hut. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, Tamayori-hime "Lady Summoned Soul", and they had four children: ''Itsuse'' "Hallowed Harvest", ''Inahi'' "Rice Spirit", ''Mikenu'' "Master Mighty Offerings and the future Emperor Jinmu, who had several names: Master Plentiful Mighty Offerings, Young Master Mighty Offerings and Boulder Hamlet Lad of Sacred Yamato. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inahi went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Itsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler of earth. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs= {{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans_title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033}} }} ==External references== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, the Lady Bountiful Soul fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" because of her birthing hut. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, Tamayori-hime "Lady Summoned Soul", and they had four children: ''Itsuse'' "Hallowed Harvest", ''Inahi'' "Rice Spirit", ''Mikenu'' "Master Mighty Offerings" and the future Emperor Jinmu, who had several names: Master Plentiful Mighty Offerings, Young Master Mighty Offerings and Boulder Hamlet Lad of Sacred Yamato. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inahi went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Itsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler of earth. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs= {{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033}} }} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto''' "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" {{nihongo|鵜葺草葺不合命|}},{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|authorlink1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} commonly shortened to '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[kami]], and in [[Japanese mythology]], he is the father of Japan's first emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'' his name appears as {{nihongo|Seaside Brave Lad of Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching|天津日高日子波限 建鵜草葦不合命|ama tsu hikohiko nagisa take u ga ya fuki aezu no mikoto}} and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' the same name appears with a different spelling. Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]] "Flickering Flame" (the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] "Ripening Rice-Ears Lad", who was sent by [[Amaterasu]] to rule the Earth), and of [[Toyotama-hime]] or "Lady Beautiful Soul", a daughter of Ocean Majesty (now identified as the dragon king, [[Ryūjin]]). Although Lady Beautiful Soul became pregnant at Ocean Majesty's undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, the parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, the Lady Beautiful Soul fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" because of her birthing hut. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, Tamayori-hime "Lady Summoned Soul", and they had four children: ''Itsuse'' "Hallowed Harvest", ''Inahi'' "Rice Spirit", ''Mikenu'' "Master Mighty Offerings" and the future Emperor Jinmu, who had several names: Master Plentiful Mighty Offerings, Young Master Mighty Offerings and Boulder Hamlet Lad of Sacred Yamato. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inahi went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Itsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler of earth. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs= {{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese |trans-title=Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033}} }} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

On the shore, the parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, the Lady Beautiful Soul fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching" because of her birthing hut. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, Tamayori-hime "Lady Summoned Soul", and they had four children: ''Itsuse'' "Hallowed Harvest", ''Inahi'' "Rice Spirit", ''Mikenu'' "Master Mighty Offerings" and the future Emperor Jinmu, who had several names: Master Plentiful Mighty Offerings, Young Master Mighty Offerings and Boulder Hamlet Lad of Sacred Yamato. Mikenu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inahi went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Itsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler of earth. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs= {{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | deadurl = yes | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | df = }} }} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

{{Nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命}},{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|authorlink1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} also known as '''Ugayafukiaezu''', is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'', and is in [[Japanese mythology]], the father of Japan's first Emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as {{Nihongo||天津日高日子波限建鵜葺草葺不合命|Amatsuhiko Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as {{Nihongo||彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊|Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]], the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who was sent down by [[Amaterasu]] to govern the earth ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]) (believed to be equivalent to Japan), and of [[Toyotama-hime]], a daughter of [[Ryūjin]], the dragon ''[[kami]]'' of the sea. Although Toyotama-hime became pregnant at the undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, her parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: ''Hikoitsuse'' , ''Inai'', ''Mikeirinu'', and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | deadurl = yes | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | df = }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. {{Nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命}}{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|authorlink1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'', and is in [[Japanese mythology]], the father of Japan's first Emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as {{Nihongo||天津日高日子波限建鵜葺草葺不合命|Amatsuhiko Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as {{Nihongo||彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊|Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]], the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who was sent down by [[Amaterasu]] to govern the earth ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]) (believed to be equivalent to Japan), and of [[Toyotama-hime]], a daughter of [[Ryūjin]], the dragon ''[[kami]]'' of the sea. Although Toyotama-hime became pregnant at the undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, her parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). ***NOTE: Hikohohodemi was actually Jimmu's grandfather and his name here is a mistake. Kamu-yamato Iware-biko no mikoto, became Emperor Jimmu Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

On the shore, her parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = File:Ugaya otokowa.png | consort = [[Tamayori-hime]] | parents = [[Hoori]] (father) [[Toyotama-hime]] (mother) | children = [[Emperor Jimmu]] }} {{Nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命}}{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|author-link1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'', and is in [[Japanese mythology]], the father of Japan's first Emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as {{Nihongo||天津日高日子波限建鵜葺草葺不合命|Amatsuhiko Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as {{Nihongo||彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊|Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. [[Basil Hall Chamberlain]] glossed the ''Kojiki'' name as "His Augustness Heaven's-Sun-Height-Prince-Wave-limit-Brave-Cormorant-Thatch-Meeting-Incompletely". Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]], the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who was sent down by [[Amaterasu]] to govern the earth ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]) (believed to be equivalent to Japan), and of [[Toyotama-hime]], a daughter of [[Ryūjin]], the dragon ''[[kami]]'' of the sea. Although Toyotama-hime became pregnant at the undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, her parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. The roof hadn't been completely thatched (''fukiaezu'') with cormorant feathers (''ugaya'') when his mother gave birth to him, which explains his name, Ugayafukiaezu. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = File:Ugaya otokowa.png | consort = [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] | parents = [[Hoori]] (father) [[Toyotama-hime]] (mother) | children = [[Emperor Jimmu]] }} {{Nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命}}{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|author-link1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'', and is in [[Japanese mythology]], the father of Japan's first Emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as {{Nihongo||天津日高日子波限建鵜葺草葺不合命|Amatsuhiko Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as {{Nihongo||彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊|Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. [[Basil Hall Chamberlain]] glossed the ''Kojiki'' name as "His Augustness Heaven's-Sun-Height-Prince-Wave-limit-Brave-Cormorant-Thatch-Meeting-Incompletely". Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]], the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who was sent down by [[Amaterasu]] to govern the earth ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]) (believed to be equivalent to Japan), and of [[Toyotama-hime]], a daughter of [[Ryūjin]], the dragon ''[[kami]]'' of the sea. Although Toyotama-hime became pregnant at the undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, her parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{quote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. The roof of the birthing hut hadn't been completely thatched (''fukiaezu'') with cormorant feathers (''ugaya'') when his mother gave birth to him, which explains his name. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = File:Ugaya otokowa.png | birth_place = [[Japan]] | birth_date = 735 BC | death_date = 640 BC (aged 115) | death_place = [[Japan]] | consort = [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] | parents = [[Hoori]] (father) [[Toyotama-hime]] (mother) | children = [[Emperor Jimmu]] }} {{Nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命}}{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|author-link1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'', and is in [[Japanese mythology]], the father of Japan's first Emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as {{Nihongo||天津日高日子波限建鵜葺草葺不合命|Amatsuhiko Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as {{Nihongo||彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊|Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==Genealogy== Ugayafukiaezu is in the [[Three generations of Hyuga]], a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. {{Three_generations_of_Hyuga}} ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==Genealogy== Ugayafukiaezu is in the [[Three generations of Hyuga]], a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}.{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}} {{Three_generations_of_Hyuga}} ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

revision.comment: Fixed mistake in math (age was 20 years off)
revision.text:
{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = File:Ugaya otokowa.png | birth_place = [[Japan]] | birth_date = 735 BC | death_date = 640 BC (aged 95) | death_place = [[Japan]] | consort = [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] | parents = [[Hoori]] (father) [[Toyotama-hime]] (mother) | children = [[Emperor Jimmu]] }} {{Nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命}}{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|author-link1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'', and is in [[Japanese mythology]], the father of Japan's first Emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as {{Nihongo||天津日高日子波限建鵜葺草葺不合命|Amatsuhiko Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as {{Nihongo||彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊|Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. [[Basil Hall Chamberlain]] glossed the ''Kojiki'' name as "His Augustness Heaven's-Sun-Height-Prince-Wave-limit-Brave-Cormorant-Thatch-Meeting-Incompletely". Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]], the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who was sent down by [[Amaterasu]] to govern the earth ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]) (believed to be equivalent to Japan), and of [[Toyotama-hime]], a daughter of [[Ryūjin]], the dragon ''[[kami]]'' of the sea. Although Toyotama-hime became pregnant at the undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, her parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{blockquote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. The roof of the birthing hut had not been completely thatched (''fukiaezu'') with cormorant feathers (''ugaya'') when his mother gave birth to him, which explains his name. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==Genealogy== Ugayafukiaezu is in the [[Three generations of Hyuga]], a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}.{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}} {{Three_generations_of_Hyuga}} ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

On the shore, her parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{blockquote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. The roof of the birthing hut had not been completely thatched (''fukiaezu'') with cormorant feathers (''ugaya'') when his mother gave birth to him, which explains his name. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==Genealogy== Ugayafukiaezu is in the [[Three generations of Hyuga]], a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}} {{Three_generations_of_Hyuga}} ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==Genealogy== Ugayafukiaezu is in the [[Three generations of Hyuga]], a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}} {{Three_generations_of_Hyuga}} ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

{{Short description|Figure in Japanese mythology and Shinto}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = File:Ugaya otokowa.png | birth_place = [[Japan]] | birth_date = 735 BC | death_date = 640 BC (aged 95) | death_place = [[Japan]] | consort = [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] | parents = [[Hoori]] (father) [[Toyotama-hime]] (mother) | children = {{Plainlist| *[[Emperor Jimmu]] * {{ill|Itsuse|ja|彦五瀬命}} *[[Inahi no Mikoto]] *{{ill|Mikeiri|ja|三毛入野命}} }} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto'''|鵜葺草葺不合命}}{{cite book|last2=Heldt|first2=Gustav|author1=Ō no Yasumaro|author-link1=Ō no Yasumaro|title=The Kojiki : an account of ancient matters|date=2014|isbn=9780231163897}} is a [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'', and is in [[Japanese mythology]], the father of Japan's first Emperor, [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Nomenclature and story== [[File:Phalacrocorax carbo ja01.jpg|thumb|right|[[Great cormorant]] with individual feathers visible, [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]]] In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', his name appears as {{Nihongo||天津日高日子波限建鵜葺草葺不合命|Amatsuhiko Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, and in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as {{Nihongo||彦波瀲武鸕鶿草葺不合尊|Hiko Nagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. [[Basil Hall Chamberlain]] glossed the ''Kojiki'' name as "His Augustness Heaven's-Sun-Height-Prince-Wave-limit-Brave-Cormorant-Thatch-Meeting-Incompletely". Ugayafukiaezu was a child of [[Hoori]], the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], who was sent down by [[Amaterasu]] to govern the earth ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]) (believed to be equivalent to Japan), and of [[Toyotama-hime]], a daughter of [[Ryūjin]], the dragon ''[[kami]]'' of the sea. Although Toyotama-hime became pregnant at the undersea palace of [[Ryūgū-jō]], she opted not to bear the child in the ocean and decided to head to shore. On the shore, her parents attempted to build a house in which she could give birth, and attempted to construct the roof with feathers of the [[cormorant]] instead of [[Cladium|saw grass]]. However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{blockquote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"

Now, thinking these words strange, he sneaked up and peered in at her just as she was about to give birth.

She had become an enormous sea beast many arm spans in length that was twisting and slithering around on its stomach.

In shock and fright at the sight of her, he immediately fled far away.}} In shame, Toyotama-hime fled, leaving behind her newborn, whom she called Ugayafukiaezu. The roof of the birthing hut had not been completely thatched (''fukiaezu'') with cormorant feathers (''ugaya'') when his mother gave birth to him, which explains his name. Later, when Ugayafukiaezu reached adulthood, he married his aunt, [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]], and they had four children: Hikoitsuse, Inai, Mikeirinu, and Hikohohodemi (later [[Emperor Jimmu]]). Mikeirinu traveled to Tokoyo, the "Everworld", and Inai went into the ocean to be with his mother. The eldest and youngest set forth to rule the land and while they did so together for a time, after Hikoitsuse died, their youngest became the first ruler. ==Genealogy== Ugayafukiaezu is in the [[Three generations of Hyuga]], a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}} {{Three_generations_of_Hyuga}} ==References== {{reflist|2|refs={{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 鵜葺草葺不合命 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-04 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Ugayafukiaezu | oclc = 683276033 | url-status = dead | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 }}}} ==External links== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 Encyclopedia of Shinto] {{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

However, while they were finishing the roof, she went into labor. {{blockquote|And so just as she was about to give birth, she spoke to her husband, saying:

"When their time draws near, people of other lands all give birth in the form of their homeland. So I will now give birth in my original form. Please, I beg you, do not look at me!"
